votes.reason: The Python code accurately translates the Coq string operations and list comprehensions, correctly implementing the prefix check, string dropping, and counting logic.
The Python code correctly implements the string dropping, prefix checking, and the exact logic of the Coq specification for counting substring occurrences.

The Python implementation correctly translates the Coq logic, with drop, is_prefixb, and how_many_times_spec matching the behavior of their Coq counterparts for all valid string and natural number inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. drop(n, s) returns s[n:] which matches the Coq behavior of dropping n characters. is_prefixb checks if t is a prefix of u, which the Python version does correctly using slicing. how_many_times_spec counts positions where substring is a prefix of the suffix starting at that position, matching the Coq filter/seq logic. The only minor difference is drop handles n <= 0 by returning s, but since seq 0 (length s) only produces non-negative indices starting from 0, this doesn't affect the result.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. drop(n, s) returns s[n:] which matches Coq's drop. is_prefixb checks if t is a prefix of u, which the Python version does correctly via u[:n] == t. how_many_times_spec counts positions where substring is a prefix of the suffix starting at that position, matching the Coq filter/length computation. The only minor difference is Python's drop handles n <= 0 by returning s, but since seq 0 (length s) only produces non-negative indices starting from 0, this doesn't affect results.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec. drop(n, s) returns s[n:] which matches Coq's drop. is_prefixb checks if t is a prefix of u, which the Python version does correctly via u[:n] == t. how_many_times_spec counts positions where substring is a prefix of the suffix starting at that position, matching the Coq filter/seq logic. The only minor difference is Python's drop handles n <= 0 by returning s, but since seq starts at 0 and goes to len(s)-1, n is always non-negative in the actual usage.

Python drop, is_prefixb, and how_many_times_spec mirror Coq behavior on all concrete inputs used (i in 0..len(s)-1), including empty substring and overlong substring cases; both count prefix matches at each position except the end.

Python drop removes first n chars (capped to empty) and is_prefixb checks t is a prefix of u via slicing; both match Coq behavior. how_many_times_spec counts positions i in 0..len(s)-1 where substring is a prefix of drop i s. All edge cases (empty strings, longer substring) align.
Python drop, prefix check, and counting over range(len(s)) match the Coq definitions’ behavior on all inputs (including empty substring and n > len(s)).
